title: Chapter 3 Master Quiz
description: Test your logic! Relational Operators, If, Else, and Logical Operators.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an `if` statement?
options: To run a block of code conditionally.
To repeat a block of code.
To print text to the screen.
questions.explanation: If statements allow your program to deviate from a straight-line path and make execution conditional.

questions.question: The condition inside an `if` statement's parentheses `()` must always evaluate to which data type?
questions.explanation: The condition must resolve to either `true` or `false`.

questions.question: Which operator checks if two values are NOT equal?
questions.explanation: `!=` means Not Equal in Java.

questions.question: What does the `==` operator do?
options: Assigns a value to a variable.
Compares two values to see if they are equal.
Checks if a value is greater than another.
questions.explanation: Double equals (`==`) compares values. Single equals (`=`) assigns values.

questions.question: If `int age = 15;`, the expression `age >= 18` evaluates to false.
questions.explanation: 15 is not greater than or equal to 18, so the expression is false.

questions.question: When does the code inside an `else` block execute?
options: When the compiler throws an error.
When the condition in the preceding `if` statement is false.
When the condition in the preceding `if` statement is true.
questions.explanation: The `else` block is the backup plan. It runs only if the `if` block's condition failed (was false).

questions.question: An `else` block cannot exist on its own without a preceding `if` block.
questions.explanation: An `else` must always follow an `if` or an `else if`. It cannot stand alone.

questions.question:
If `int score = 85;` what will this print? 
`if (score > 90) { print("A"); } else { print("B"); }`
questions.explanation: 85 > 90 is false, so the execution falls into the `else` block.

questions.question: In an `if - else if - else` chain, how many blocks can execute?
options: All of them if their conditions are true
questions.explanation: The chain is mutually exclusive. As soon as Java finds the FIRST true condition, it executes that block and ignores the rest of the chain.

questions.question: The logical AND operator is written as _____
questions.explanation: The double ampersand (`&&`) is the logical AND operator in Java.

questions.question: The logical OR operator is written as _____
questions.explanation: Two vertical pipes (`||`) represent logical OR in Java.

questions.question: For `A && B` to be true, BOTH A and B must be true.
questions.explanation: The AND block only evaluates to true if every condition connected by `&&` is true.

questions.question: For `A || B` to be true, BOTH A and B must be true.
questions.explanation: With the OR operator (`||`), if AT LEAST ONE of the conditions is true, the whole statement is true.

questions.question: What does the `!` (NOT) operator do?
options: It flips the boolean to its opposite value.
questions.explanation: NOT flips the truthiness. `!true` becomes `false`, and `!false` becomes `true`.

questions.question: What is the result of `!(5 == 5)`?
questions.explanation: `5 == 5` is `true`. The `!` operator flips it to `false`.

questions.question: Which of these expressions is true?
questions.explanation: 10 is less than OR EQUAL TO 10. This makes it true.

questions.question: If `x = 5`, what will `if (x = 10)` do?
questions.explanation: A single `=` is an assignment operator, which does not return a boolean. `if()` requires a boolean expression (so it should be `x == 10`).

questions.question: Consider `true || false`. What is the result?
questions.explanation: In an OR (`||`) expression, if ANY part is true, the result is true.

questions.question: Consider `true && false`. What is the result?
questions.explanation: In an AND (`&&`) expression, ALL parts must be true for the result to be true.

questions.question: Which is valid syntax?
questions.explanation: In Java, it must be exactly `else if (condition)` with a space between the words.

questions.question: If multiple `else if` conditions in a chain are true, which blocks will run?
options: All of the blocks that are true.
Only the very first one that is true.
Only the very last one that is true.
questions.explanation: Java evaluates from top to bottom. Once it finds a true condition, it runs that block and skips all remaining `else if` and `else` blocks in the chain.
